questions.description: (Fictional) Directive (EU) 2021/7777 of the European Parliament and of the… Directive, Director.
questions.text: (Fictional) Directive (EU) 2021/7777 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 25 April 2021 on measures for a high common level of cybersecurity across the Union provides, amongst other things, the following:- Article 7: 'Each Member State shall adopt a national cybersecurity strategu with a view to achieving and maintaining a high level of cybersecurity [including] a plan to enhance the general level of cybersecurity awareness among citizens' and Article 44: 'By 26 June 2022, Member States shall adopt and publish the measures necessary to comply with this Directive. They shall immediately inform the Commission thereof. They shall apply those measures from 27 June 2022. Ireland has not published or adopted a national cybersecurity strategy by March 2023. There is also currently no plan published that is aimed at enhancing the general level of cybersecurity awareness campaign run by CyberTrust in the national media since May 2022 designed to highlight the most commonly experienced cybersecurity issues. CyberTrust are a private organisation who are co-funded equally by a group of private computer manufacturers based in Ireland and the governmental Department of the Environment, Climate and Communications. The Minister also appoints the Executive Director of CyberTrust, who in turn has to report back to the Minister on the activities of CyberTrust on an annual basis. Harry is a 77 year old man who lives in Galway and has only started using computers and the internet in recent months. Last month, he was sent an email about a lottery win in Canada, which he could claim by sending in his credit card details -- which he did. The email was fraudulent and Harry's credit card was subsequently used by the fraudsters to make purchases amounting to €4,800. Harry is licensed. He claims to have known nothing about the potential for email to be used to phish for his credit card details in this way and insists that the State should be doing more to protec vulnerable and inexperienced internet users from this type of activity. Referring to relevant caselaw of the CJEU throughout your answer, advise Harry on whether he can possibly use the provisions set out in Articles 7 and 44 of the fictional Directive (EU) 2021/7777 to assist his claim for compensation from the State for the financial losses that he has suffered.

questions.description: Explain, with reference to relevant case-law of the CJEU throughout your answer, how the status of human rights has…
questions.text: Explain, with reference to relevant case-law of the CJEU throughout your answer, how the status of human rights has changed within the EU legal order since its foundation in the 1950s [and]{.underline} how the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights has, since it became legally binding, now become the main basis upon which the CJEU ensures that human rights are observed in the Union legal order.

questions.description: Outline, by reference to relevant TEU and TFEU Treaty provisions and pertinent case-law of the Union judicature, the…
questions.text: Outline, by reference to relevant TEU and TFEU Treaty provisions and pertinent case-law of the Union judicature, the roles of each of: (i) the European Parliament; (ii) the Commission; [and]{.underline} (iii) the Council in establishing new or amending legislative, delegated and implementing acts. Assess also whether the changes made to legislative procedures by the Lisbon Treaty amendments have improved the democratic legitimacy of decision-making within the European Union.

questions.description: There is a shortage of second-hand cars available in Ireland at present.
questions.text: There is a shortage of second-hand cars available in Ireland at present. As a result, second-hand cars are now imported from other EU Member States at record high levels --- rising from an average of 1,000 imported EU cars per year in 2021 up to 15,000 imported EU cars in 2022. Before exportation, these cars are adapted from right sided driving to left sided driving specifically for the Irish market. The Irish Revenue Commissioners have adapted the method used to calculate vehicle registration tax (VRT) on these imported EU cars during 2022. They have added the cost of adapting the vehicles for the Irish market and they have added a new carbon emission cost account for the work required to make these modifications to the imported EU cars. As a result, imports of second-hand cars from other EU Member States have become significantly more expensive in Ireland. For example, the average VRT on a 2015 Renault Clio 1.4 which has been driven fo approximately 100,000 kms was €1,600 in 2021. The same car, imported from another EU Member State is now charged a VRT on importation of €1,500, plus 35% of the modification cost (35% of €2,000 = €700) plus the new carbon emission cost of carrying out these modifications (€300), raising the VRT due in 2023 to a total of €2,500. A group of both Irish importers and EU exporters have come together to raise this issue with the EU Commission, claiming that the new system. of calculating these vehicle registration taxes on modified second-hand EU cars breaches TFEU rules on taxation, as set out in Article 110 TFEU. Referring to relevant CJEU case-law throughout your answer, advise the EU Commission on whether it should consider bringing legal action against Ireland due to these changes to the Revenue Commissioners' methods and rates of calculating VRT in this way. Your advice should also address the pre-litigation steps that the EU Commission would have to follow prior to initiating an action under Article 258 TFEU against Ireland.

questions.description: Sylvester is a French national who moved to Dublin in March 2019.
questions.text: Sylvester is a French national who moved to Dublin in March 2019. Initially, he was not successful in securing employment in Ireland but during this period he lived with his brother who did not charge him any rent or for any utility bills and he lived off his own modest savings for nearly a full year. In March 2020, Sylvester did obtain some casual work in a launderette, where he still works. There are no guaranteed hours of work. Some weeks Sylvester does not get any work at all, but most weeks he at least gets some hours of paid work, usually in a range between 8 and 14 hours for which he is currently paid at the national minimum wage of €11.30 per hour. He has continued to live with his brother and now makes some financial contributions towards utility bills. In August 2022 Sylvester applied for a place on a two-year college course in animation, starting in September of that year in Art College Dublin. The enrolment fees for both Irish and EU students are €1,500 per year. Irish students earning below €300 per week at the time of their application are also entitled to apply for a State-provided maintenance grant towards their living costs of €3,000 per year plus payment of 50% of their enrolment fees. Sylvester has applied for the State-provided maintenance grant and enrolment fee reduction. His application has been denied because the scheme is currently only open to Irish applicants. Sylvester has now been sent a bill for the full €1,500 enrolment fee, payable immediately at the risk of being expelled from the College. He has also had to cut back on his hours of work at the launderette since starting the course in animation due to his college commitments related to full-time attendance requirements and coursework completion. Referring to relevant CJEU case-law and EU legislation throughout your answer, advise Sylvester on whether he can bring a legal challenge about the compatibility of this student mainte:iance and fee-reduction scheme with relevant aspects of EU law.

questions.description: With reference to relevant CJEU case-law and Commission decisions, analyse critically the extent to which Article 106…
questions.text: With reference to relevant CJEU case-law and Commission decisions, analyse critically the extent to which Article 106 TFEU provides for an exception to the application of EU competition law, particularly Articles 101 and 102 TFEU, and the scope and limits to any such exception as regards public undertakings, undertakings to which Member States have granted special or exclusive rights and undertakings entrusted by Member States with the operation of services of general economic interest.

questions.description: Write a case-note on any [THREE]{.underline} of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each case-…
questions.text: Write a case-note on any [THREE]{.underline} of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each case-note: (a) the core facts; (b) the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration by the CJEU; and (c) how those issues were addressed by the CJEU (d) its judgment: (i) C-267/91, Keck and Mithouare ECLI:E:L:C:1993:905; (ii) C-352/98, Laboratoires Pharmaceutiques Bergaderm v Commission ECLI:EU:C:2000:361; (iii) C-384/93, Alpine Investments v Minister van Financien ECLI:EU:C:1995:126; (iv) 46/76, Bauhuis v, Netherlands ECLI:EU:C:1977:6; (v) 6/64, Costco v ENEL ECLI:EU:C:1964:66; (vi) 96/85, Jenkins v Kingsgate ECLI:EU:C:1981:80; (vii) C-.200/02, Zhu and Chen v Secretary of State for Home Dept. ECLI:EU:C:2004:639; (viii) C-378/17, Minister for Justice and Commissioner of An Garda Siochana v Workplace Relations Commission ECLI:EU:C:2018:979.

questions.description: Considering [both]{.underline} (i) the amendments that have been made to the former Article 230(4) EC by Article 263(4)…
questions.text: Considering [both]{.underline} (i) the amendments that have been made to the former Article 230(4) EC by Article 263(4) TFEU with regard to certain Union acts; and (ii) judicial attempts that have been made to reform the test for individual concern in particular, analyse critically whether it has become any easier in 2023 for non-privileged applicants to seek judicial review of EU acts than it was when Advocate General Jacobs delivered his opinion on Case C-50/00, UPA v Council [2002] ECR 1-6677. Your answer should include an analysis of the CJEU case-law interpreting Article 263(4) TFEU.

questions.description: On 10 May 2023, Directive 2023/970 of the European Parliament and of the… Directive.
questions.text: On 10 May 2023, Directive 2023/970 of the European Parliament and of the Council was published in the Official Journal of the EU. The Directive is designed to strengthen the application of the principle of equal pay for equal work or work of equal value between men and women. The Directive entered into force on 6 June 2023 and all EU Member States will have to implement all of the provisions of the Directive by 7 June 2026. The Directive includes several provisions designed to guarantee pay transparency in both the public and the private sectors. Article 5(2) of the Directive specifically provides that: an employer shall not ask applicants about their pay history during their current or previous employment relationships. The Irish Minister for Enterprise, Trade and Employment has issued a [fictional] press statement stating that Article 5(2) of Directive 2023/970 will not be transposed into Irish Law as knowing about the pay history of a successful job applicant is crucial to negotiating and determining what salary level should be set in a public sector post and is essential to ensuring that public funds are protected. A private employers' group has also expressed concerns about Article 5(2) of the Directive, stating that it should not apply in the private sector either as it will lead to wage inflation across all sectors. A [fictional] trade union, the Irish Worker Solidarity Association (IWSA), has 80,000 members in Ireland across both the public and private sectors. The IWSA has come to you for advice on whether the Irish Government may be compelled to transpose all provisions of Directive 2023/970, including Article 5(2) thereof, in full by 7 June 2026. Referring to relevant CJEU case-law on the Treaty obligations of Member States to transpose Union directives and on the capacity for direct effect of such directives throughout your answer, advise the Irish Worker Solidarity Association on this point.

questions.description: In Case C-235/17, Commission v Hungary ECLI:EU:C:2019:423, the CJEU reminded us that: [...
questions.text: In Case C-235/17, Commission v Hungary ECLI:EU:C:2019:423, the CJEU reminded us that: [... ] it should be borne in mind that the fundamental rights guaranteed by the Charter are applicable in all situations governed by EU law and that they must, therefore, be complied with inter alia where national legislation falls within the scope of EU law. [para. 63] In this Judgment, the CJEU was addressing issues related to Article 17 of the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union on the right to property. With reference to the case-law of the CJEU concerning the Charter throughout your answer, assess how the CJEU in that case-law, since the Charter was declared in December 2000, but especially since it came into force on 1 December 2009 along with the Treaty of Lisbon, has allowed provisions of the Charter to be used to challenge a wide range of both EU and national legislative measures in the manner described in Commission v Hungary (above).

questions.description: Outline, by reference to relevant TEU and TFEU Treaty provisions and pertinent case-law of the Union judicature, the…
questions.text: Outline, by reference to relevant TEU and TFEU Treaty provisions and pertinent case-law of the Union judicature, the roles of each of: (i) the European Parliament; (ii) the Commission; and (iii) the Council in establishing new or amending legislative, delegated and implementing acts.

questions.description: Paolo is an Italian national who is married to an Irish national, Pauline. Advise Paolo about whether EU law on free…
questions.text: Paolo is an Italian national who is married to an Irish national, Pauline. The couple left Italy in 2016 to move to Ireland as Pauline was offered a job in Dublin. Paolo has never worked in Ireland. They have two children, Paul and Paula, who were both born in Italy in 2013 and who also resided in Italy until 2016. The children are both Italian/Irish dual nationals. Shortly after the family moved to Ireland, Paolo and Pauline separated. They divorced in 2021. Paolo, who still resides with Pauline, Paul and Paula in Ireland to be near his children as they grow up, has become almost deaf in his left ear and his hearing is also deteriorating in his right ear. The condition is inoperable, and Paolo was informed by his audiologist in March 2023 that the only solution to help his worsening hearing is the use of hearing aids. Paolo, who is still not working, cannot afford to pay for the hearing aids, which in his case will cost at least €1,900, has now applied to get a financial grant towards this cost under the Treatment Benefit Scheme, which is run in Ireland by the Department of Social Protection. The grant, however, is only available to insured workers, the self-employed and retired people who have the required number of social insurance (PRSI) contributions. Under the Scheme, the Department will pay a grant once every four years of up to €1,000 towards a pair of hearing aids if required. While Paolo has not worked in Ireland, a dependant spouse, civil partner or cohabitant can still qualify for treatment benefit on the spouse's Social Insurance record. Pauline has made sufficient Social Insurance contributions since 2016. Paolo has applied to the Department of Social Protection to become affiliated to the Treatment Benefit Scheme. This request was refused in April 2023 on the ground that he was neither employed nor self-employed nor retired in Ireland, and he was no longer the spouse of a worker who qualified for the Treatment Benefit Scheme. Paolo appealed this decision to an appeal panel at the Department of Social Protection. The appeal was dismissed in July 2023. Paolo has since been engaged in illegal activity in a bid to fund the cost of his new hearing aids. He was caught driving off a car ferry in Dublin Port in late July 2023 with a consignment of 20,000 counterfeit cigarettes, which were destined for the Irish black market. He also resisted arrest at the time, including punching the ribs of a member of An Garda Síochána who had arrived to assist. Paolo received a suspended sentence of two years' imprisonment when he was before the Circuit Court last month, as this was just his second offence --- he was also convicted of shoplifting when he was on holidays in Ireland in 2012, for which he was fined €300. However, on this occasion, the Minister for Justice has also ordered Paolo's deportation from Ireland as part of a concerted Government campaign against counterfeit cigarette smuggling. Paolo now wants to challenge: (i) the deportation order made against him; and (ii) the refusal to accept him on to the Treatment Benefit Scheme. He claims that as an EU citizen, with children residing in Ireland, and having been married to a worker through whom he should be qualified for the Treatment Benefit Scheme, that he is entitled to remain in Ireland and to receive the hearing aid grant. Referring to relevant CJEU case-law and TFEU Treaty provisions and EU legislation throughout, advise Paolo about whether EU law on free movement and citizenship may help his challenges against both the deportation order and the refusal to include him in the Treatment Benefit Scheme.

questions.description: The French Government has recently introduced a [fictional] law, No. Company.
questions.text: The French Government has recently introduced a [fictional] law, No. 23-77, that all new light fittings sold in France after 31 December 2023 must be automatic --- switching on when someone enters a room or other lit space and then switching themselves off 90 seconds after everyone has left a room or lit space. The French Government has deemed that this is a necessary part of reducing overall energy consumption in France as part of national energy security policy and to contribute to reducing the country's carbon emissions. Brillo Bulbs Ltd. is a company based in Blessington, County Wicklow, which manufactures light fittings, mostly for export. France is the company's second biggest market (34% of all of its sales) after Germany (40% of all of its sales). No Brillo Bulbs Ltd. products are automatic. All require a manual switching on and a manual switching off. Brillo Bulbs Ltd. wants to complain to the EU Commission about this new [fictional] French law. Brillo Bulbs Ltd. insists that the operation of lights, manual and automatic combined, accounts for less than 2% of France's overall energy consumption. Referring to relevant TFEU provisions and case law of the CJEU throughout, advise Brillo Bulbs Ltd. on the compatibility, or otherwise, of [fictional] French Law No. 23-77 with EU rules on the free movement of goods.

questions.description: The Irish Government has become concerned about the bad publicity that…
questions.text: The Irish Government has become concerned about the bad publicity that Ireland has been receiving as a destination for tourists due to a number of stories that have appeared in the international media about physical attacks on foreign tourists and also about how expensive hotel rooms in Ireland are and about the consistently bad weather during the summer months. As a response, the Irish Minister for Tourism, Culture, Arts, Gaeltacht, Sport and Media has proposed a number of [fictional] measures designed to make Ireland a more attractive holiday destination for overseas travellers. These proposals include: - (i) a further cut to the Value Added Tax (VAT) rate for the hospitality and tourism sectors from the standard rate of 13.5%, currently already reduced to 9%, but now to be further reduced to 5%; a subsidy to be paid to all Irish-owned airlines of €5.70 per passenger travelling on all inbound flights with these Irish-owned airlines to airports in Ireland; (iii) an interest-free loan scheme that can be used by tourist attractions to assist with their public liability insurance costs. A [fictional] Government Department for Tourism, Culture, Arts, Gaeltacht, Sport and Media report, published in early September 2023, and based on interviews with overseas travellers to Ireland during the summer of 2023, found that incoming tourist numbers would likely decline by 3-5% during the summer of 2024, primarily due to the high cost of hotel accommodation, poor weather and embassy advice about the frequency of physical attacks on overseas tourists. Several operators, including a German airline, a French hotel group and a private body which represents the hospitality sector in Spain, have raised concerns regarding the proposals listed above with the EU Commission, claiming that, if implemented, they would amount to unlawful State Aid. Referring to relevant legal sources throughout, write a report for the EU Commission, assessing whether or not these Irish Ministerial proposals may amount to State aid under Article 107 TFEU and, if so, whether they may, nonetheless, be considered thereunder, as well as under relevant EU legislative provisions on State aid as interpreted in the relevant case-law of the Union judicature and by reference to any applicable EU Commission notices applying those provisions, to be compatible with the internal market.

questions.description: Write a case-note on any THREE of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each case-note: (a) the…
questions.text: Write a case-note on any THREE of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each case-note: (a) the core facts; (b) the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration by the CJEU; and (c) how those issues were addressed by the CJEU in its judgment: (i) Case C-212/97, Centros Ltd v Erhvervs- og Selskabsstyre/sen [1999] ECR 1 1459 [MAR 22, MAR 21, MAR 20, OCT 19]; (ii) c-352/98, Laboratoires Pharmaceutiques Bergaderm v Commission ECLI:EU:C:2000:361 [MAR 23, MAR 20]; (iii) Case 33/74, Van Binsbergen v Bestuur van de Bedrijfsvereniging voor de Metaalnijverheid [1974] ECR 1299 [OCT 22]; (iv) case 140/79, Chemial Farmaceutici v DAF SpA [1981] ECR 1 [MAR 20, OCT 19]; (v) Case 87/75, Bresciani v Amministrazione Italiana del/e Finanze [1976] ECR 129 [OCT 22, OCT 20]; (vi) 6/64, Costa v ENEL ECLI:EU:C:1964:66 [MAR 23, OCR 22]; (vii) Case C-236/09, Association belge des Consommateurs Test-Achats v Conseil des ministres EU:C:2011:100 [OCT 22, MAR 22, MAR 20]; (viii) Cases 56 and 58/64, Établissements Consten SARL and Grundig-Verkaufs-GmbH v Commission [1966] ECR 299 [OCT 20].

questions.description: A [fictional] AgriSci report published in October 2022 concluded that the… Regulation.
questions.text: A [fictional] AgriSci report published in October 2022 concluded that the consumption of the [fictional] food additive E1111, which is a flavouring that is primarily used in raspberry cream biscuits, has a number of side effects in children. This includes causing hormonal changes in male teenagers and hyperactivity in younger children. The EU Commission have acted on the basis of these conclusions and published a proposal for a new [fictional] EU Regulation 5555/2023, which will ban the use of E1111 at any level in food production. This Regulation 5555/2023 is due to come into full effect by 31 October 2025. AgriSci is a very well-respected and independent food science organisation, with a reputation for only employing the very best scientists. Belters Ltd. is an Irish food-additives manufacturer. In September 2023, it produced 88% of all E1111 used in the manufacture of products containing this additive across the EU. Given the popularity of raspberry cream biscuits, the sale of E1111 by Belters Ltd. accounts for an average of 65% of its total annual revenue over the past five years. Belters Ltd. has come to you for advice on how it could challenge the introduction of EU Regulation 5555/2023. In particular, it is concerned that it may have no legal standing even to bring a challenge before the Union judicature on the introduction of Regulation 5555/2023. It has commissioned its own expert report, which has come to different conclusions to those presented in the AgriSci report of October 2022. The Belters Ltd. report has concluded that, while there may be some side effects to consuming E1111, young persons would have to consume unrealistically copious quantities of this additive (the equivalent of that contained in at least one hundred raspberry cream biscuits per day, every day) before they could possibly suffer any detrimental impact to their health. Advise Belters Ltd. on whether it would have locus standi to bring a challenge to the legality of Regulation 5555/2023 before the General Court. Your answer should include a detailed analysis of the relevant case-law of the Union judicature interpreting Article 263(4) TFEU and how it relates to the admissibility of an action like that being considered by Belters Ltd.
